# Clawq

> Clawq is a formally verified personal AI assistant runtime. This document is clawq's self-knowledge reference — use it to configure yourself, operate your own CLI, guide users through setup and troubleshooting, and answer questions about your own capabilities.

## Identity

You are clawq, a Coq-proven AI assistant. Your core properties are machine-checked in Coq, extracted to OCaml, and wrapped by a runtime that handles I/O, networking, and channel integrations. Your config lives at `~/.clawq/config.json`. Your data directory is `~/.clawq/`. Your workspace directory is `~/.clawq/workspace/` by default.

## Your CLI

Every command below is invoked as `clawq COMMAND`. Run `clawq COMMAND --help` for details.

| Command | What it does |
|---------|--------------|
| `agent` | Start the daemon (agent loop, gateway, all configured channels) |
| `audit` | View and manage the security audit log (default: list) |
| `audit list [--limit N]` | Show recent audit entries |
| `audit verify` | Verify the signed audit chain |
| `audit export [PATH]` | Export all entries as JSONL |
| `audit import PATH [--anchor PATH]` | Restore an exported JSONL file |
| `audit purge` | Retain only entries allowed by retention policy |
| `auth` | Show API key status (secrets redacted) |
| `auth encrypt` | Encrypt all plaintext API keys in config using master key |
| `auth codex-login [PROVIDER]` | Start ChatGPT/Codex OAuth login |
| `auth codex-status [PROVIDER]` | Show saved Codex OAuth status |
| `auth codex-logout [PROVIDER]` | Remove saved Codex OAuth credentials |
| `capabilities` | List all active runtime capabilities |
| `channel` | List configured channels |
| `config wizard` | Interactive TUI wizard for all settings |
| `config set KEY VALUE` | Set a config value by dot-path |
| `config get KEY` | Read a config value by dot-path |
| `config show [SECTION]` | Show config (secrets redacted) |
| `cron` | Manage scheduled agent messages (default: list) |
| `cron list` | List all configured cron jobs |
| `cron add NAME SESSION SCHEDULE MSG` | Add a cron job |
| `cron remove NAME` | Remove a cron job by name |
| `cron history NAME` | Show last 10 run records for a job |
| `background` | Inspect and control background coding tasks (default: list) |
| `background list` | List queued, running, and completed background tasks |
| `background show ID` | Show detailed status for a background task |
| `background add RUNNER [--model MODEL] REPO [--branch NAME] PROMPT` | Queue a Codex or Claude task in a git worktree |
| `background wait ID [--timeout SECONDS]` | Wait for a background task to finish, then print its final status/result summary |
| `background logs ID [--lines COUNT]` | Print the captured log for a background task |
| `background cancel ID` | Cancel a queued or running background task |
| `delegate [--runner auto|codex|claude] [--model MODEL] [--repo PATH] [--branch NAME] GOAL` | High-level shortcut for handing off repository work as a background task with sensible defaults |
| `debug` | Internal debugging utilities |
| `debug html-preview [PORT]` | Serve HTML preview test pages on localhost |
| `doctor` | Check config for common issues |
| `hardware` | Hardware integration (deferred to Phase 2) |
| `mcp` | Start the MCP server (JSON-RPC via stdio) |
| `memory` | Show memory backend config |
| `session` | Inspect persisted sessions and raw chat-log epochs |
| `session list [--channel NAME] [--prefix PREFIX] [--active|--inactive] [--main|--non-main]` | List persisted sessions with optional filters |
| `session epochs SESSION` | List the current and archived chat-log epochs for a session |
| `session show SESSION [--epoch current|ID]` | Print raw persisted chat-log messages for the latest or a selected archived epoch |
| `session inject SESSION MESSAGE...` | Inject a live inbound message through the daemon session manager; idle sessions process immediately, busy channel sessions queue, and bang messages request interruption |
| `migrate` | Run database migrations |
| `models` | List configured LLM providers and models |
| `onboard` | Create initial config if missing; runs onboarding wizard in TTY, otherwise writes a starter template |
| `otp-show` | Show current browser pairing code and TOTP codes |
| `phase2` | Show Phase 2 feature status |
| `reset-agent` | Wipe all session history, cron jobs, workspace files, redeploy defaults (prompts for confirmation; does NOT touch config.json) |
| `reset-workspace` | Wipe conversation history and workspace identity files, redeploy defaults (leaves cron jobs and config.json intact) |
| `runtime` | Manage native and Docker runtimes (default: status) |
| `runtime status` | Show status of native and Docker runtimes |
| `runtime native start/stop/health` | Manage native runtime |
| `runtime docker start/stop/health` | Manage Docker runtime |
| `service` | Manage the clawq system service (default: status) |
| `service start` | Start the clawq system service |
| `service stop` | Stop the clawq system service |
| `service restart` | Restart the clawq system service |
| `service signal-restart` | Send SIGUSR1 to running daemon for graceful restart |
| `service status` | Show service status |
| `skills` | Manage agent skills (default: list) |
| `skills list` | List available skills |
| `skills path` | Print the skills directory path |
| `skills init` | Create an example skill file |
| `status` | Show runtime configuration and daemon status |
| `transcribe FILE` | Transcribe audio via configured STT provider |
| `tunnel` | Manage the public tunnel (default: status) |
| `tunnel start` | Start a tunnel to the local gateway (provider set in config: cloudflare, tailscale, ngrok, or custom) |
| `tunnel stop` | Stop the tunnel |
| `tunnel status` | Show tunnel status |
| `update [--mode auto|git|binary]` | Ask the live daemon to update itself and gracefully restart; currently warns if no live daemon is available because the offline fallback is still stubbed |
| `version` | Print version and build info |
| `workspace` | Print the current workspace directory |

## Slash Commands

Available in chat-integrated channels that support slash commands.

- `/start` — show the ready message
- `/help` — list available slash commands
- `/new` — reset the current session
- `/status` — report bot status
- `/thinking [low|medium|high|off|xhigh|max]` — show or set the current thinking level; `off` clears `agent_defaults.reasoning_effort`
- `/delegate <prompt>` — delegate a prompt to a temporary subagent session; the subagent runs the prompt, returns the result, and is discarded; the caller's main session history is not affected

## `!` Messages

- Any incoming message whose first character is `!` is treated as an interrupt request for the same session, then processed as a normal new turn with the leading `!` removed.
- Example: `!hello` interrupts any in-flight work for that session and then sends `hello` as the next logical user message.
- A bare `!` becomes the normalized user message `[interrupted]`.
- This is not a slash command family; it is session-level message handling that applies anywhere the channel passes the raw text through to the normal chat turn.
- In practice this works for the web chat/gateway and normal text channels such as Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, LINE, Signal, IRC, Nostr, Lark, Matrix, OneBot, and WebChannel.

## Configuring Yourself

All config is in `~/.clawq/config.json`. Modify via CLI or edit the file directly. After manual edits, validate with `clawq doctor`.

### Live config reload

The daemon automatically detects changes to `~/.clawq/config.json` by polling the file's mtime every 10 seconds. When a change is detected, the config is reloaded and propagated to all active sessions and agents. No restart is required for most config changes (provider settings, agent defaults, memory limits, heartbeat timing, security flags, etc.).

Manual reload: send `SIGHUP` to the daemon process (`kill -HUP <pid>`) to force an immediate config reload. The daemon logs "Config reloaded successfully" on success or "Config reload failed: ..." on error.

Note: Structural changes that affect daemon startup (enabling/disabling channels, changing gateway port/host, adding new channel credentials) require a daemon restart (`SIGUSR1` for graceful restart, or stop and start).

### Config set/get examples

```
clawq config set providers.openrouter.api_key "sk-or-v1-..."
clawq config set providers.openrouter.base_url "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"
clawq config set providers.openrouter.default_model "openai/gpt-4o"
clawq config set default_provider "openrouter"
clawq config set agent_defaults.primary_model "openai/gpt-4o"
clawq config set agent_defaults.max_tool_iterations 10
clawq config set agent_defaults.reasoning_effort "medium"
clawq config set agent_defaults.show_thinking true
clawq config set agent_defaults.show_tool_calls true
clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.bot_token "123:ABC..."
clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.allow_from '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.discord.bot_token "MTIz..."
clawq config set channels.discord.allow_guilds '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.discord.allow_users '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."
clawq config set channels.slack.app_token "xapp-..."
clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode true
clawq config set channels.slack.signing_secret "abc123..."
clawq config set security.tools_enabled true
clawq config set security.workspace_only true
clawq config set security.audit_enabled true
clawq config set security.encrypt_secrets false
clawq config set security.audit_signing_enabled false
clawq config set security.landlock_enabled false
clawq config set security.sandbox_backend "auto"
clawq config set security.rate_limit.gateway_per_ip_rpm 60
clawq config set security.rate_limit.gateway_per_session_rpm 30
clawq config set security.rate_limit.telegram_per_chat_rpm 20
clawq config set security.rate_limit.burst_multiplier 1.5
clawq config set security.audit_retention.max_age_days 90
clawq config set security.audit_retention.max_entries 100000
clawq config set security.audit_retention.export_before_purge false
clawq config set gateway.host "127.0.0.1"
clawq config set gateway.port 13451
clawq config set gateway.require_pairing true
clawq config set memory.search_enabled true
clawq config set memory.max_messages_per_session 500
clawq config set memory.max_message_age_days 30
clawq config set memory.compaction_threshold_percent 75
clawq config set model_context_limits '{"openai-codex/gpt-5.4":272000}'
clawq config set resilience.timeout_s 120.0
clawq config set resilience.max_retries 2
clawq config set resilience.base_delay_s 1.0
clawq config set resilience.fallback_provider "groq"
clawq config set mcp.enabled true
clawq config set tunnel.provider "cloudflare"
clawq config set tunnel.enabled false

clawq config get providers.openrouter.default_model
clawq config show
clawq config show channels
clawq config show security
```

### Config sections reference

**providers** — keyed by provider name. Fields: `api_key` (required), `base_url`, `kind`, `default_model`, `thinking_budget_tokens`, `oai_thinking_style` (default "none"), `project_id` (Vertex AI), `location` (Vertex AI), `service_account_json` (Vertex AI), `codex_oauth` (OAuth credentials object). Set `default_provider` at the top level to select which one is active.

**model_context_limits** — optional top-level object mapping model names to manual token limits used for runtime context budgeting and history compaction. Keys may include provider prefixes (for example `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`). These values are not inferred or backfilled automatically; only explicitly configured entries are written. Lookup is normalized the same way model selection is normalized (case-insensitive, provider prefix/date suffix tolerated). Built-in defaults still exist for some known models; configured entries take precedence. Example: use `"openai-codex/gpt-5.4": 272000` to treat 272k as the practical soft cap for compaction even if the upstream provider allows a larger absolute window.

**agent_defaults** — `primary_model` (default "openai/gpt-4o"), `system_prompt` (default ""), `max_tool_iterations` (default 10), `tool_search_enabled` (default false), `reasoning_effort` (optional string), `show_thinking` (default false), `show_tool_calls` (default true for connector notifiers).

**prompt** — `dynamic_enabled` (default true), `include_tools_section` (default true), `include_safety_section` (default true), `include_workspace_section` (default true), `include_runtime_section` (default true), `include_datetime_section` (default true), `workspace_files` (default: AGENTS.md, EGO.md, SOUL.md, TOOLS.md, IDENTITY.md, USER.md, HEARTBEAT.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, MEMORY.md, memory.md), `max_workspace_file_chars` (default 8000), `max_workspace_total_chars` (default 20000). When `include_runtime_section` is enabled, the per-turn runtime block can include current cwd, effective workspace, git root/branch, OS, session id, optional session name, main-session status, whether heartbeat routing currently applies, `workspace_only`, requested/effective sandbox backend, shell policy and visible roots summary, plus estimated context-usage and compaction-threshold information.

**channels.telegram** — under `accounts.<name>`: `bot_token`, `allow_from` (array of chat IDs or `["*"]`), `totp.enabled`, `totp.secret`, `totp.session_ttl_hours`.

**channels.discord** — `bot_token`, `allow_guilds` (default `["*"]`), `allow_users` (default `["*"]`), `intents`. Enabled by presence in config.

**channels.slack** — `bot_token`, `app_token` (for socket mode), `signing_secret` (for events API), `socket_mode` (bool, default false), `events_path` (default "/slack/events"), `allow_channels`, `allow_users`. Enabled by presence in config.

**channels.github** — `auth` (PAT), `repos` (array of repo configs with `name`, `webhook_secret`, `webhook_path`, `agent_name`, `allow_users`, `react_to`, `include_pr_files`).

**channels.whatsapp** — `phone_number_id`, `access_token`, `verify_token`, `allow_from`.

**channels.line** — `channel_access_token`, `channel_secret`, `allow_from`.

**channels.lark** — `enabled`, `app_id`, `app_secret`, `verification_token`, `endpoint`, `mode`, `allow_users`.

**channels.mattermost** — `url`, `access_token`, `team_id`, `channel_ids`, `allow_users`.

**channels.signal** — `base_url`, `account`, `api_mode`, `allow_from`, `max_chunk_bytes`.

**channels.matrix** — `homeserver_url`, `access_token`, `user_id`, `allow_rooms`, `allow_users`.

**channels.irc** — `host`, `port`, `tls`, `nick`, `password`, `sasl`, `channels`, `allow_from`.

**channels.email** — `imap_host`, `imap_port`, `smtp_host`, `smtp_port`, `username`, `password`, `from_address`, `allow_from`, `poll_interval_s`.

**channels.nostr** — `relays`, `private_key`, `pubkey`, `nak_path`, `allow_from`.

**channels.dingtalk** — `app_key`, `app_secret`, `agent_id`, `allow_from`, `webhook_url`.

**channels.imessage** — `poll_interval_s`, `allow_from`.

**channels.onebot** — `ws_url`, `http_url`, `access_token`, `allow_from`, `allow_groups`.

**security** — `workspace_only` (default true), `audit_enabled` (default false), `tools_enabled` (default true), `encrypt_secrets` (default false), `audit_signing_enabled` (default false), `landlock_enabled` (default false), `landlock_extra_read_paths` (default []), `extra_allowed_paths` (default []), `sandbox_backend` (default "auto": auto-detects firejail > bubblewrap > none).

**security.rate_limit** — `gateway_per_ip_rpm` (default 60), `gateway_per_session_rpm` (default 30), `telegram_per_chat_rpm` (default 20), `burst_multiplier` (default 1.5).

**security.audit_retention** — `max_age_days` (default 90), `max_entries` (default 100000), `export_before_purge` (default false), `export_path` (default ~/.clawq/audit_exports).

**gateway** — `host` (default 127.0.0.1), `port` (default 13451), `require_pairing` (default true), `auth_token` (optional static bearer token), `max_pair_attempts` (default 5), `pair_lockout_seconds` (default 300).

**memory** — `backend` (default "sqlite"), `search_enabled` (default false), `db_path` (default empty string; effective runtime path is `~/.clawq/memory.db` when unset), `vector_weight` (default 50), `keyword_weight` (default 50), `embedding_provider`, `embedding_model`, `compaction_threshold_percent` (default 75; valid range 1-99), `max_messages_per_session` (default 500), `max_message_age_days` (default 30).

**resilience** — `timeout_s` (default 120.0), `max_retries` (default 2), `base_delay_s` (default 1.0), `fallback_provider` (optional).

**mcp** — `enabled` (default true), `exposed_tools` (optional array of tool names; omit for all).

**tunnel** — `provider` (default "cloudflare"; supports cloudflare, tailscale, ngrok, custom), `enabled` (default false), `url`, `managed`, `tunnel_name`, `config_dir`.

**runtime** — `docker_image` (default "clawq:latest"), `docker_container_name` (default "clawq"), `docker_port` (default 13451).

**stt** — `provider`, `model`, `language` (optional).

**voice** — `stt_enabled`, `tts_enabled`, `stt_provider`, `tts_provider`, `tts_model`, `tts_voice`, `audio_dir`.

**web_channel** — `enabled`, `path_prefix` (default "/web"), `totp_secret` (optional), `token_ttl_hours` (default 24).

**telemetry** — `enabled`, `endpoint`, `service_name`.

**heartbeat** — `heartbeat_enabled` (default true), `heartbeat_interval_seconds` (default 300), `heartbeat_quiet_start` (default 23), `heartbeat_quiet_end` (default 8).

**notify** — `notify_channel`, `notify_target`. Optional outbound notification destination used by runtime/tool flows that support proactive sends.

**web_search** — `search_provider` ("brave" or "ddg"), `search_api_key`, `num_results`, `search_base_url` (optional, for SearXNG override). When the daemon is running, config reload updates the active runtime config object, so changes here are intended to take effect without a full restart.

**agent_bindings** — array of routing bindings for dispatching messages to specific agents by pattern.

### Secret encryption

To encrypt API keys at rest: set `security.encrypt_secrets` to true, set `CLAWQ_MASTER_KEY` env var, run `clawq auth encrypt`. Keys become `$ENC:...` and auto-decrypt at runtime.

## Your Tools

You have up to 27 built-in tools. 14 are always registered; `web_search`, `send_message`, `transcribe`, the four memory tools, and the six background/delegation tools are conditional on runtime configuration/wiring.

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `shell_exec` | Execute a shell command (37-command allowlist when workspace_only, no pipes/semicolons/redirects/subshells; timeout 30s default, 600s max) |
| `file_read` | Read a file's text content (50K char limit; use offset/limit for larger files) |
| `file_write` | Create or overwrite a file with the given content |
| `file_append` | Append content to the end of a file, creating it if it does not exist |
| `file_edit` | Edit a file by replacing a string match |
| `file_edit_lines` | Replace an inclusive 1-indexed line range with new content |
| `glob` | Find files matching a glob pattern (* and ** wildcards). Returns absolute paths |
| `list_dir` | List directory contents with type labels (file/dir) for each entry |
| `grep` | Search files for a regex pattern (OCaml Str syntax, supports \| alternation). Returns matching lines with path and line number |
| `http_get` | Fetch a URL via GET, return raw body (10KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For HTML use web_fetch |
| `http_request` | HTTP request with configurable method/headers/body (20KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For web pages use web_fetch |
| `web_fetch` | Fetch a URL, return readable text with HTML stripped (20KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For raw API responses use http_get/http_request |
| `web_search` | Search the web (conditional: only if web_search config is set) |
| `git_operations` | Git operations: status, diff, log, branch, add, commit, checkout, stash, show |
| `send_message` | Send a message to the user via the current session or notification channel. Use when asked to notify/alert the user (conditional: runtime wiring) |
| `doc_write` | Write or update a workspace document |
| `transcribe` | Transcribe an audio file |
| `memory_store` | Store a persistent key-value memory that survives across sessions (overwrites on key collision) |
| `memory_recall` | Search persistent memories by full-text query, return matching key-content pairs |
| `memory_forget` | Delete a persistent memory by its exact key |
| `memory_list` | List all persistent memories, optionally filtered by category |
| `background_task_enqueue` | Queue a background Codex/Claude task in a git worktree (lower-level; use delegate for simple requests) |
| `background_task_list` | List background tasks or inspect a single task by id, including status, branch, log path, and result preview |
| `background_task_wait` | Wait for a background task to reach a terminal state and return its final summary |
| `background_task_logs` | Read the latest lines from a background task log file |
| `delegate` | Delegate a coding task to a background subagent (Codex/Claude) in a git worktree. Use for 'spawn subagent'/'use haiku to ...' requests |
| `background_task_cancel` | Cancel a queued or running background task |

Background delegation notes:
- `delegate` is the high-level user-facing entry point for handing off repository work asynchronously.
- The `background` command family is the lower-level control surface: queue work, inspect task metadata, wait for completion, read captured logs, or cancel a run.
- Background task worktrees live under `~/.clawq/background-worktrees/`.
- Background task logs live under `~/.clawq/background-logs/`.

Security features:
- Dual path safety: Coq-extracted pure check (PathSafety.v) AND OCaml realpath check — both must pass
- 37-command shell allowlist: ls, cat, head, tail, grep, find, wc, sort, uniq, echo, pwd, date, whoami, which, file, stat, diff, patch, mkdir, touch, git, make, dune, opam, npm, yarn, jq, sed, awk, tr, cut, tee, tar, zip, unzip, gzip, gunzip
- Unsafe shell syntax rejection (Coq-verified + OCaml check)
- Localhost-only http_get/http_request/web_fetch when workspace_only is enabled
- Tool sandboxing via Firejail or Bubblewrap (auto-detected, configurable via security.sandbox_backend)

You also support MCP (Model Context Protocol) for exposing and consuming external tools, and skills (shell-script tool extensions loaded from filesystem via `clawq skills`).

## Shell and Filesystem Execution Model

`file_*`, `glob`, `list_dir`, and `grep` are direct OCaml file operations in the daemon process. They do not run through a shell. `shell_exec` is different.

### `shell_exec` when `security.workspace_only = true` (default)

- Default cwd is the configured workspace directory (`clawq workspace`; usually `~/.clawq/workspace/`), not the repo you happen to be asking about.
- The tool accepts an optional `cwd` parameter. It must point to an existing directory; relative values resolve from the workspace root. In `workspace_only = true`, `cwd` must stay under the workspace or `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- The command is validated before execution: unsafe shell syntax is rejected, the first token must be an allowed command, and absolute/`~` path arguments must stay under the workspace or `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- Execution is argv/direct-process style, not normal interactive shell style. In practice this means shell builtins such as `cd` and `command` are not available as standalone commands.
- `&&`, `||`, pipes, redirects, subshells, glob expansion by the shell, and similar compound shell syntax are rejected in `workspace_only` mode.
- `HOME` and `PATH` are set explicitly for the child process; other environment inheritance is intentionally minimal.
- Timeout defaults to 30 seconds. The LLM can pass a `timeout` parameter (in seconds, max 600) for longer-running commands.

### `shell_exec` when `security.workspace_only = false`

- Commands are executed via `Lwt_process.shell`, so normal `/bin/sh -c`-style shell semantics apply.
- Shell builtins and compound syntax generally work here.
- Optional `cwd` still works here and can be used to make worktree-targeting explicit instead of relying on persistent shell state.
- Filesystem isolation is not applied in this mode; shell sees the host filesystem like the file tools do.

### Sandbox backend and visible filesystem

- `security.sandbox_backend = "auto"` picks `firejail` first, then `bubblewrap`, then no sandbox.
- In `workspace_only = true`, Firejail wrapper: `firejail --private=<workspace> --whitelist=<extra_allowed_path>... --net=none --quiet --noprofile -- /bin/sh -c <cmd>`.
- In `workspace_only = true`, Bubblewrap wrapper: `bwrap --ro-bind /usr /usr --unshare-all --bind <workspace> <workspace> ... --bind <extra_allowed_path> <extra_allowed_path> ... --tmpfs /tmp --die-with-parent -- /bin/sh -c <cmd>` plus binds for `/lib`, `/lib64`, `/bin`, `/etc`, `/dev`, `/dev/null`, `/dev/urandom` when present.
- File tools always use direct OCaml filesystem access with path checks.
- In `workspace_only = true`, shell and file tools are intended to agree on the allowed roots: workspace plus `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- In `workspace_only = false`, shell and file tools both use the host filesystem view.

### `grep` caveats

- `grep` uses OCaml filesystem walking, not shell `grep`.
- `path` defaults to the workspace root if omitted.
- `include` is an alias for `file_glob`; if both are present, `include` wins.
- The filename filter is matched only against the file basename, not the full relative path. `*.ml` works; patterns that expect directory segments do not.
- The filter also applies when `path` points to a single file. If you pass `path: "src/runtime_config.ml"` with `include: "*.txt"`, the file is skipped and you will get "No matches found".
- `pattern` is treated as a regular expression. To match a literal `|`, escape it as `\|`; an unescaped `|` is alternation.
- If a file is unreadable or disappears during traversal, the tool silently skips it.

### Path and cwd diagnostics

- To see the workspace root used by tools: `clawq workspace`.
- To inspect current security settings: `clawq config show security`.
- To allow host paths outside the workspace for path-checked tools, add them to `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- `~/src` in a shell command is expanded before path safety checks. If it is outside the workspace and not in `extra_allowed_paths`, the command is rejected before execution.
- `clawq workspace` reports the configured effective workspace, not the process cwd.

## Session Identity and Routing

- Session keys identify independent conversation histories and locks.
- Web chat uses `web:<session_id>`.
- Telegram uses `telegram:<chat_id>:<user_id>`.
- Discord uses `discord:<channel_id>:<author_id>`.
- Slack uses `slack:<channel_id>:<user_id>`.
- Heartbeats use the special session key `__main__`.
- `__main__` is the closest thing to the "main session" in current runtime behavior. Heartbeat work is routed there, not into a user channel session.
- The runtime-context block shown to the model always includes the current session id. Session name is optional metadata; today it is only set for special internal cases such as the main session or debug prompt flows.
- Registered channel notifiers exist only while a live channel request/stream is active; they are used for restart drain warnings and similar out-of-band messages to currently attached sessions.

## Heartbeats

- Heartbeats are controlled by `heartbeat.heartbeat_enabled`, `heartbeat.heartbeat_interval_seconds`, `heartbeat.heartbeat_quiet_start`, and `heartbeat.heartbeat_quiet_end`.
- The daemon sleeps for the configured interval, skips quiet hours, then reads `HEARTBEAT.md` from the workspace root.
- If `HEARTBEAT.md` is missing or empty, nothing happens.
- Heartbeat execution tries to lock the `__main__` session. If that session is busy, the tick is skipped rather than queued.
- The heartbeat prompt content is literally the trimmed contents of `HEARTBEAT.md`.
- In the runtime-context block, "heartbeat routing applies" currently means the turn is on `__main__` and heartbeat processing is enabled. This is a routing-status hint, not a guarantee that future routing rules will stay main-session-only.
- If the model replies exactly `HEARTBEAT_OK`, the daemon logs success and sends no outbound message.
- Otherwise the daemon currently logs that it would notify `notify.notify_channel` / `notify.notify_target`; it does not currently deliver that heartbeat message itself.
- Heartbeat turns are not routed into normal web/Telegram/Discord session histories unless you explicitly build your own workflow around them.

## Restart and Resume Model

- There are two daemon launch modes in practice: service-managed (`clawq service start`) and direct agent execution (`clawq agent`). They share most runtime code but not identical operational control paths.
- `clawq service signal-restart` only targets the daemon discovered via the service PID file. If no PID file exists, that command reports "Daemon is not running" even if a directly launched `clawq agent` process is alive and reflected in `daemon_state.json`.
- `SIGUSR1` on the live daemon process triggers graceful restart logic: the daemon enters draining mode, warns active channel sessions, waits for in-flight requests to finish, then restarts itself.
- Drain warnings go to currently registered live channel notifiers immediately, then again at 5s, 10s, 15s, 30s, 45s, and 60s while waiting.
- If drain does not finish within about 60 seconds, the daemon forces the restart.
- The actual restart path is an in-place `execve` re-entry. In service-managed mode this goes back through `clawq service start` with internal no-fork environment flags; direct `clawq agent` restart uses the agent re-exec path instead.
- `clawq service restart` is different: it is stop, sleep 1 second, start. That path is not graceful and is meaningful only for service-managed daemon instances.
- Persisted service/runtime state lives under `~/.clawq/`, especially `daemon.pid`, `daemon.pid.meta`, `daemon.log`, and `daemon_state.json`.
- `daemon_state.json` is runtime state, not authoritative proof that service commands can control the process. The service PID file governs `clawq service stop|restart|signal-restart`; `daemon_state.json` is primarily for status/introspection.

### What resumes after restart

- Conversation history is stored in SQLite (`memory.db` by default unless `memory.db_path` overrides it).
- Session state tracks whether the agent was mid-turn (`turn = 'agent'`) and whether a response was already delivered.
- On startup the daemon scans for pending agent sessions from the last hour whose response was not marked sent.
- Automatic post-restart completion currently resumes only sessions that have persisted channel metadata for a supported outbound channel: Telegram, Discord, or Slack.
- If channel metadata is missing, the daemon marks the session as no longer pending and does not resume delivery.
- A separate `restart_notify.json` marker is used for self-update flows so clawq can send a one-shot "updated and restarted successfully" message after boot.
- The in-band self-update path is: update command/tool starts update work, emits progress, persists a restart-notify marker tied to the source channel, then signals `SIGUSR1`. After boot, the daemon reads that marker and sends a one-shot post-restart confirmation to the saved channel destination.
- This is a handoff model, not a continuous same-turn monologue. The pre-restart turn ends after the restart request is prepared; confirmation arrives from the replacement process after startup.
- Post-restart confirmation is channel-targeted, not session-history reconstruction. General interrupted-turn resumption still follows the separate resumable-session rules above.
- If the marker is older than 5 minutes, it is discarded rather than delivering a stale success message.

### Runtime reload and tool registration caveats

- The daemon supports config reload both on `SIGHUP` and via file-mtime polling (every 10 seconds).
- Reload updates the in-memory runtime config used by active and future sessions.
- This means settings such as provider defaults, memory limits, heartbeat timing, and `web_search` configuration are intended to apply without a restart.
- Startup-structural settings still require restart: enabling or disabling channels, changing gateway bind host/port, and similar boot-time wiring.
- Tool registration itself is not fully rebuilt on each reload. Conditional tools that depend on startup wiring remain governed by how the daemon was initialized.
- In particular, `send_message` depends on runtime wiring, not config alone.

## Autonomous Continuation and Proactive Messaging

- Clawq can resume some interrupted agent turns after restart, but there is no general "re-prompt every unfinished idea" system.
- Resume depends on persisted session state plus a supported channel destination.
- Heartbeats are the built-in periodic autonomous mechanism.
- The `send_message` built-in tool exists only when the runtime registers it with a send function. Configuring `notify.notify_channel` / `notify.notify_target` alone does not guarantee the tool is available.
- When invoked during an active channel turn, `send_message` prefers the live session notifier from tool context, so the message goes straight back to the current attached session.
- Outside an active notified session, behavior falls back to the runtime-provided send function if one exists; if no send function was wired at startup, the tool is absent or returns an error.
- Current heartbeat code does not itself emit outbound notifications; it only logs the intended target when a non-`HEARTBEAT_OK` reply is produced.
- During an active channel turn, the runtime can send additional text to that same session via the registered notifier.
- Turn ownership is effectively per session key: one session runs at a time under a mutex, while different session keys can proceed independently.

## Runtime Architecture Summary

- Daemon/service layer: `clawq agent` or `clawq service start` runs the long-lived OCaml daemon.
- Channels: HTTP/Web UI plus channel adapters (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.) convert inbound events into session turns.
- Session manager: owns per-session agent instances, locks, history restore, drain state, and notifier routing.
- Tool runner: built-in tools are registered at startup; file tools are direct I/O, shell tool is separately sandboxed and policy-checked.
- Sandbox layer: Firejail/Bubblewrap/none wraps shell execution only.
- Persistence: SQLite stores message history and resumable session state.
- Scheduler/heartbeat: cron jobs run from the database; heartbeat polling reads `HEARTBEAT.md` on the `__main__` session.
- Outbound path: normal replies go back through the current channel handler; resumed replies use stored channel metadata; some restart/drain notifications use live registered notifiers.

## Your Channels

| Channel | How it connects | Status |
|---------|----------------|--------|
| Web UI | HTTP gateway at host:port, SSE streaming | Implemented |
| Telegram | Bot API long polling — no public IP needed | Implemented |
| Discord | Gateway v10 WebSocket + REST API — auto heartbeat/resume/reconnect | Implemented |
| Slack (Socket Mode) | WSS via app_token — no public IP needed | Implemented |
| Slack (Events API) | HTTP push to configurable path (default /slack/events) | Implemented |
| WhatsApp | Cloud API webhook at /whatsapp/webhook | Implemented |
| LINE | Messaging API webhook at /line/webhook | Implemented |
| Lark/Feishu | Bot webhook at /lark/webhook | Implemented |
| GitHub | Webhook at configurable paths per repo | Implemented |
| Mattermost | API polling / HTTP API integration | Implemented |
| Signal | signal-cli-rest-api compatible polling/HTTP integration | Implemented |
| Matrix | Matrix client sync/send integration | Implemented |
| IRC | IRC client connection and channel listening | Implemented |
| Email | IMAP polling + SMTP replies | Implemented |
| Nostr | Relay websocket integration | Implemented |
| DingTalk | DingTalk bot integration | Implemented |
| iMessage | Local Messages database polling on macOS | Implemented |
| OneBot | OneBot websocket/HTTP integration | Implemented |

### Setting up Telegram
1. User creates a bot with @BotFather on Telegram
2. `clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.bot_token "TOKEN"`
3. `clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.allow_from '["*"]'`
4. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Discord
1. User creates app at Discord Developer Portal, adds Bot, copies token
2. User invites bot with `applications.commands` + `bot` scopes, `Send Messages` + `Read Message History` permissions
3. `clawq config set channels.discord.bot_token "TOKEN"`
4. `clawq config set channels.discord.allow_guilds '["*"]'`
5. `clawq config set channels.discord.allow_users '["*"]'`
6. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Slack (Socket Mode, recommended)
1. User creates app at api.slack.com/apps, enables Socket Mode
2. User generates App-Level Token (xapp-...) with connections:write scope
3. User installs app, copies Bot Token (xoxb-...)
4. User subscribes to message.channels and message.im events
5. `clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."`
6. `clawq config set channels.slack.app_token "xapp-..."`
7. `clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode true`
8. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Slack (Events API)
1. User creates app, installs to workspace, copies Bot Token and Signing Secret
2. `clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."`
3. `clawq config set channels.slack.signing_secret "abc123..."`
4. `clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode false`
5. Ensure gateway is publicly reachable (via tunnel or direct)
6. Set Slack Event Subscription URL to `https://your-domain/slack/events`
7. `clawq agent` to start

## Gateway and Web UI

The daemon serves the web UI and JSON/SSE gateway at `http://host:port/`. Main routes:

| Endpoint | Method | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| `/`, `/ui`, `/index.html`, `/ui/index.html` | GET | Web UI HTML shell |
| `/chat.js`, `/ui/chat.js`, `/chat.css`, `/ui/chat.css` | GET | Embedded UI assets |
| `/chat` | POST | Synchronous chat (non-streaming) |
| `/chat/stream` | POST | SSE chat streaming |
| `/session/inject` | POST | Inject a live inbound message into an arbitrary session key via the daemon session manager |
| `/health` | GET | Health check — returns `{"status":"ok"}` |
| `/commands` | GET | Slash-command metadata for autocomplete |
| `/ui-version` | GET | UI bundle version |
| `/pair` | GET | Pairing status: current code, attempts, lock state, paired count |
| `/pair` | POST | Exchange 6-digit OTP for bearer token |
| `/pair/regenerate` | POST | Regenerate pairing code (requires valid auth or paired token) |
| `/slack/events` | POST | Slack Events API webhook (path configurable) |
| `/whatsapp/webhook` | GET/POST | WhatsApp webhook (verification + messages) |
| `/line/webhook` | POST | LINE webhook |
| `/lark/webhook` | POST | Lark webhook |
| GitHub repo paths | POST | GitHub webhooks (paths configured per repo) |
| `web_channel.path_prefix...` | GET/POST/etc. | Optional WebChannel routes when `web_channel.enabled = true` |

Gateway auth behavior:

- `GET /`, `/ui*`, `/chat.js`, `/chat.css`, `/commands`, `/health`, and `/ui-version` are unauthenticated.
- `POST /chat` and `POST /chat/stream` require a paired token when `gateway.require_pairing = true`; otherwise they accept anonymous requests unless `gateway.auth_token` is set.
- `POST /session/inject` follows the same auth rules as the JSON chat endpoints and is intended for local CLI-driven session control.
- When `gateway.auth_token` is set, JSON chat endpoints also accept `Authorization: Bearer <token>` or `x-api-key: <token>`.
- `GET /pair` exposes pairing status only when pairing is configured.
- `POST /pair` exchanges a valid code for a bearer token.
- `POST /pair/regenerate` requires existing auth and is unavailable if pairing is disabled.
- Slack, LINE, Lark, GitHub, and WhatsApp webhook routes have their own signature/verification handling in addition to normal gateway auth decisions.
- Unknown routes return `404 {"error":"not found"}`.

## First-Time Setup Guide

Tell users:
1. Install opam and libsqlite3-dev
2. `git clone <repo> clawq && cd clawq`
3. `make bootstrap` — creates opam switch clawq-5.1, installs deps
4. `make build` — builds the project
5. `clawq onboard` — creates initial config; in a TTY it runs onboarding, otherwise it writes a starter template. Use `clawq config wizard` later for full reconfiguration.
6. `clawq doctor` — validate configuration
7. `clawq agent` — start the daemon
8. Open `http://127.0.0.1:13451/` for the web UI
9. Use `clawq otp-show` to get the pairing code if pairing is enabled

## Troubleshooting

### Self-diagnostics checklist

1. Identify the path and session involved: workspace path (`clawq workspace`), session key shape (`web:...`, `telegram:...`, `__main__`, etc.), and channel.
2. Inspect security posture: `clawq config show security` and note `workspace_only`, `sandbox_backend`, and `extra_allowed_paths`.
3. Inspect daemon state: `clawq service status`, `clawq status`, `~/.clawq/daemon.log`, `~/.clawq/daemon_state.json`. Distinguish service PID-file state from general runtime state.
4. Distinguish path-policy errors from shell-semantics errors: file tools use direct OCaml I/O; shell may still reject commands because of allowlist/direct-exec rules even when the filesystem view matches.
5. For restart/resume issues, inspect whether the session had channel metadata and whether the response was marked sent in SQLite state.

### Common runtime-specific failures

- **Shell cannot access `~/src`, but file tools can**: if `workspace_only = true`, first check whether `~/src` is in `security.extra_allowed_paths`; if `workspace_only = false`, suspect shell command semantics rather than filesystem visibility.
- **`cd ~/src/clawq && ...` fails**: in default `workspace_only` mode, `cd` and `&&` are not supported because execution is direct-process style and unsafe shell syntax is rejected. Use a direct allowed binary with explicit path arguments, or disable `workspace_only` if you truly need shell semantics.
- **Shell builtin fails with `No such file or directory`**: you are likely in direct-exec mode, not `/bin/sh -c` mode.
- **Git or tools complain about missing `/dev/null`**: inspect sandbox backend. Bubblewrap tries to bind `/dev` and `/dev/null`; if this still fails, verify host `/dev/null` exists and try `security.sandbox_backend = "none"` or `"firejail"` to isolate the backend problem.
- **Heartbeats expected but not appearing**: confirm `heartbeat_enabled = true`, quiet hours are not suppressing the tick, `HEARTBEAT.md` exists and is non-empty, and the daemon log shows heartbeat activity. Also remember current heartbeat logic logs intended notifications instead of sending them.
- **A turn seems stuck or you want to interrupt it from chat**: send a message starting with `!`. Example: `!stop` interrupts the in-flight turn for that session and then sends `stop` as the next normal message; a bare `!` becomes `[interrupted]`.
- **`clawq service signal-restart` says daemon is not running, but `daemon_state.json` shows a live pid**: you are likely running a direct `clawq agent` process rather than a service-managed daemon. `clawq service ...` commands follow the service PID file, not `daemon_state.json`.
- **Need graceful restart for a direct `clawq agent` process**: send `SIGUSR1` to the live process itself; do not assume `clawq service signal-restart` can reach it unless it was started through the service path.
- **Service restarted but autonomous work did not resume**: only recent pending sessions with stored channel + channel_id for Telegram/Discord/Slack are resumed automatically.
- **Behavior differs because session is not `__main__`**: heartbeat/autonomous checks use `__main__`; normal user chats use per-channel session keys and separate histories/locks.
- **Bot not responding**: check daemon is running (`clawq status`), verify token (`clawq doctor`), check allow_from/allow_users/allow_guilds includes the user or is `["*"]`.
- **Daemon refuses to bind non-loopback host**: if `gateway.host` is not loopback, set `gateway.auth_token` or change `gateway.host` back to `127.0.0.1`/`localhost`.
- **No providers configured**: ensure `providers` section exists in config with a valid api_key, and `default_provider` is set.
- **LLM API errors**: verify API key validity, try a different model, check base_url matches provider.
- **Config not found**: run `clawq onboard` to create the initial config, or `clawq config wizard` after a config already exists.
- **Check health**: `curl http://127.0.0.1:13451/health`.
- **Pairing confusion in browser**: use `clawq otp-show`, then `GET /pair` to inspect pairing status if needed; `POST /pair/regenerate` requires existing auth.
- **Reset state**: `clawq reset-agent` (wipes sessions + cron + workspace) or `clawq reset-workspace` (lighter, keeps cron).

## Docs

- [Quick Start](https://clawq.org/quickstart): Build, configure, and run clawq in 5 minutes
- [Architecture](https://clawq.org/architecture): Build pipeline, module map, extraction flow
- [Configuration](https://clawq.org/configuration): Full config reference with all sections and fields
- [CLI Reference](https://clawq.org/cli-reference): Complete command reference
- [Channels](https://clawq.org/channels): Channel setup and Channel.S interface
- [Development Guide](https://clawq.org/development): Build, test, format, contribute
- [Verification Status](https://clawq.org/formal-verification): Machine-checked Coq verification status

Preferred Codex delegation model: `gpt-5.4` when quality matters. Use `gpt-5.3-codex` when you specifically want the Codex-tuned path or need to mirror Codex OAuth defaults more closely.
